coredata.dc:title: Ratiometric fluorescent probes based on carbon dots and europium for rapid detection of tetracycline
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Zhuoyuan
Wang, Yulin
Chen, Jinting
Zhang, Li
Hua, Yongbiao
Huang, Donglan
Guo, Huishi
Qiu, Xiuzhen
coredata.dc:description: Tetracycline (TC) is a typical emerging pharmaceuticals and personal care products (PPCPs) pollutants in the environment. However, TC residues cause severe effects on human health. It is crucial to exploit simple, rapid and sensitive methods to detect TC in water samples. A ratiometric fluorescent probes (CDs-Eu3+) is constructed using carbon dots (CDs) and europium ions (Eu3+) for the visual detection of tetracycline (TC). Under 390 nm excitation, CDs-Eu3+ shows a strong yellow fluorescence at 545 nm and a weak red fluorescence at 619 nm, respectively. In the presence of TC, the fluorescence intensity at 545 nm decreased while the fluorescence intensity at 619 nm increased, which attributed to the internal filtering effect (IFE) and “antenna effect”, respectively. Sensing performance of CDs-Eu3+ probes is further evaluated with respect to various conditions, including salt concentration, Eu3+ concentration, pH, and reaction time. Results reveal that the proposed ratiometric fluorescent probes exhibits a specific recognition ability toward TC. An excellent linear relationship is observed between the fluorescence intensity ratio (F619/F545) and TC concentrations (1⁓100 μM), with a low detection limit (LOD) of 31 nM. The solution (CDs-Eu3+-TC) color gradually changes from yellow to red after adding TC. The probes are successfully applied to determine TC in water with recoveries of 85.43⁓97.53%. Finally, a fluorescent test strip is prepared and used for rapid visual semi-quantitative TC detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots (CDs)
Tetracycline (TC)
Visual detection
Fluorescent test strip

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization and recognition mechanism of fluorescent probes
CDs -Eu3+ ratiometric fluorescent test strips for semi-quantitative detection of TC

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Detection principle and application of the ratiometric fluorescent probes.

Excitation wavelengths dependent fluorescence spectra (A) and TEM image (B) of CDs. Excitation wavelengths dependent fluorescence spectra (C) and EDS spectrum (D) of CDs-Eu3+-TC. Conditions: 75 μL of a 50-fold diluted CD solution; 100 μL Eu3+ (50 mM); 1000 μL TC solution (50 μM); Reaction time: 10 min.

UV–vis absorption spectra (A) and fluorescence spectrum (B) of CDs, CDs-Eu3+, CDs-Eu3+-TC, TC, Eu3+-TC and Eu3+. Zeta potential values (C) and fluorescence decay curves (D) of CDs, CDs-Eu3+ and CDs-Eu3+-TC. Conditions: 75 μL of a 50-fold diluted CD solution; 100 μL Eu3+ (175 mM); 1000 μL TC solution (50 μM); Reaction time: 10 min; Excitation wavelength: 390 nm.

The effect of NaCl concentration (A, B), Eu3+ concentration (C, D), and pH (E, F) on fluorescence spectra (A, C, E) and I619/I545 values (B, D, F). Conditions: Excitation wavelength: 390 nm. Inset: CDs-Eu3+-TC in different concentration of NaCl (0⁓100 mM), different concentration of Eu3+ (0⁓200 mM) and different pH values under 365 nm UV light.

The fluorescence spectrogram of CDs-Eu3+ after adding different TC concentrations (0⁓100 μM) (A). Linear relationships between I619/I545 values and TC concentrations (B). Conditions: 75 μL of a 50-fold diluted CD solution; 100 μL Eu3+ (175 mM); 1000 μL TC solution (0⁓100 μM); Reaction time: 10 min; Excitation wavelength: 390 nm; Inset: CDs-Eu3+ in different concentrations of TC (0⁓100 μM) under 365 nm UV light.
The I619/I545 value for CDs-Eu3+ after adding different interfering substances (A) and photographs of test paper based on CDs-Eu3+ (B).
Detection of TC in spiked tap and lake water samples by the proposed CDs-Eu3+ method and the HPLC method.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: was figured means of a t-test statistical analysis between two methods. When P > 0.05, there is no significant difference.

head.ce:title: Ratiometric fluorescent probes based on carbon dots and europium for rapid detection of tetracycline

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Utilization and Conservation of Food and Medicinal Resources in Northern Region, School of Chemistry and Civil Engineering, Shaoguan University, Shaoguan, 512005, PR China

School of Chemistry and Civil Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Joint Lab of Shaoguan Ecological Environment Monitoring Center and Shaoguan University, Shaoguan University, Shaoguan, 512005, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering, Hanyang University, 222 Wangsimni-Ro, Seoul, 04763, South Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering, Hanyang University, 222 Wangsimni-Ro, Seoul 04763, Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Tetracycline (TC) is a typical emerging pharmaceuticals and personal care products (PPCPs) pollutants in the environment. However, TC residues cause severe effects on human health. It is crucial to exploit simple, rapid and sensitive methods to detect TC in water samples. A ratiometric fluorescent probes (CDs-Eu3+) is constructed using carbon dots (CDs) and europium ions (Eu3+) for the visual detection of tetracycline (TC). Under 390 nm excitation, CDs-Eu3+ shows a strong yellow fluorescence at 545 nm and a weak red fluorescence at 619 nm, respectively. In the presence of TC, the fluorescence intensity at 545 nm decreased while the fluorescence intensity at 619 nm increased, which attributed to the internal filtering effect (IFE) and “antenna effect”, respectively. Sensing performance of CDs-Eu3+ probes is further evaluated with respect to various conditions, including salt concentration, Eu3+ concentration, pH, and reaction time. Results reveal that the proposed ratiometric fluorescent probes exhibits a specific recognition ability toward TC. An excellent linear relationship is observed between the fluorescence intensity ratio (F619/F545) and TC concentrations (1⁓100 μM), with a low detection limit (LOD) of 31 nM. The solution (CDs-Eu3+-TC) color gradually changes from yellow to red after adding TC. The probes are successfully applied to determine TC in water with recoveries of 85.43⁓97.53%. Finally, a fluorescent test strip is prepared and used for rapid visual semi-quantitative TC detection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid economic development, water resource pollution has become more serious, especially from pharmaceuticals and personal care products (PPCPs) [1]. Currently, PPCPs have been widely considered as emerging pollutants. Tetracycline (TC) is a typical drug as an emerging PPCPs pollutant in the environment [2]. TC is broad-spectrum antibiotics with high antimicrobial efficiency and low cost, widely used in treating bacterial infections. However, TC residues cause severe effects on human health, such as allergic reactions, gastrointestinal disturbances, hepatotoxicity, and other adverse physiological reactions [3]. Additionally, TC residues cause serious environmental concerns since they promote bacterial resistance to biotin. Therefore, TC testing is urgently needed to protect human health and the environment.

Currently, several methods have been developed for TC detection, including high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [4,5] and enzyme immunoassay [6,7]. Although these methods exhibit good performance (high sensitivity and accuracy), they are costly, time-consuming, and require expensive instrumentation and specific skilled operations [8]. Therefore, developing a low-cost, rapid visual method for detecting TC is essential. To achieve this goal, fluorescent sensors are preferred due to their simplicity, low cost, and rapid response in detecting environmental contaminants. To date, many fluorescent materials for TC detection have been reported, such as metal-organic framework materials (MOFs) [9,10] and carbon dots [11]. However, MOFs are poorly dispersed in aqueous solutions [12].

Carbon dots (CDs), a kind of emerging fluorescent carbon-based materials with diameters less than 20 nm [13–17], have attracted extensive attention due to their tunable and stable fluorescence, water solubility, good biocompatibility, and low toxicity [18,19]. For example, CDs have been used for the detection of metals [20,21], dyes [22,23], environmental pollutants [24–26], and so on. Ratiometric fluorescent probes emit two fluorescent peaks at different wavelengths under the same excitation wavelength, and their ratio is taken as the fluorescent signal [27–29]. Generally, two fluorescent materials with different fluorescence are used as fluorescence reference signals. In the presence of the target, it enhances the fluorescence of one material and maintains the fluorescence of the other material constant or decreases. Thus, an evident color change is observed by the naked eye. The europium ion (Eu3+) has an incompletely filled 4f electron layer; therefore, coordination with oxygen and nitrogen atoms is easy, forming coordination compounds with unique fluorescence properties (bright visible light emission) [30]. It has been reported that europium ion (Eu3+) emits bright red light at 619 nm after coordination with TC [31,32]. Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity of Eu3+ is rather weak due to the burst effect caused by the vibrational modes of the coordinated water molecules.

This work presents a paper-based ratiometric fluorescence probes based on CDs-Eu3+ mixing for TC detection. In the presence of TC, the yellow fluorescence of CDs is decreased based on the internal filtration effect, while the fluorescence intensity at 619 nm increases by the “antenna effect”. Therefore, TC is detected by the change in fluorescence intensity ratio between CDs and Eu3+. The CDs-Eu3+ ratio fluorescence test paper is also prepared and used for semi-quantitative visual detection of TC in environmental water samples (see Fig. 1 ).

P-phenylenediamine, chlortetracycline hydrochloride (CTC), oxytetracycline (OTC), tetracycline hydrochloride (TC), glutathione (GSH), l-cysteine (L-Cys), l-glutamic acid (L-Glu), l-histidine (L-His), l-pyroglutamic acid (L-Pyr), citric acid (CA), bovine serum albumin (BSA), europium acetate (Eu(OOCCH3)3), and 4-(2-Hydroxyethyl)-1-piperazineethanesulfonic acid (HEPES) were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). NaOH, HCl, HNO3, FeSO4·7H2O, FeCl3·6H2O, NaCl, ZnCl2, CoCl2, MgCl2, and CuSO2 were obtained from Sinopharm Group Chemical Reagent Ltd China.

UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence spectrum were recorded on a Mapada UV-650 (Shanghai, China) and an F-380 Molecular fluorescence spectrometer (Tianjin, China), respectively. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were taken with an FEI Tecnai G2 F20 X-TWIN transmission electron microscope (FEI, USA). EDS mapping was performed on a Zeiss Sigma 300 SEM. The zeta potential was tested on a Malvern Zetasizer Nano As (Mastersizer, UK). All photos were taken by a ZF-5 portable UV (365 nm) using a Xiaomi phone. An LC-2050 HPLC system (Shimadzu, Japan) with a UV detector was utilized to detect the TC concentration. The following conditions for chromatographic separation were utilized: An Agilent ZORBAX SB column (250 mm × 4.6 mm, 5 μm), a detection wavelength of 360 nm, a mobile phase comprising of acetonitrile: 0.2% acetic acid aqueous solution (15:85, v:v) at a flow rate of 1.0 mL/min.

First, 10 mL 0.14 M p-phenylenediamine and 10 mL 0.35 M nitric acid solutions were homogeneously mixed and added to a 50 mL polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) reactor. The mixture was reacted hydrothermally at 200 °C for 2 h. After cooling to room temperature, the as-prepared suspension was filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane to remove the precipitation. Then, the obtained solution was mixed with ethyl acetate at a 1:1 vol ratio and shaken mechanically for 5 min. Carbon dots were extracted into the ethyl acetate phase. It is noted that a little amount of water can be resolved by ethyl acetate. Then, n-hexane was added to the extracted supernatant (V(supernatant): V(hexane) = 5:1), shaken for 5 min, and allowed to for 10 min, sinking and stratifying the small droplets (aqueous solution of carbon dots).

The purified CDs solution (100 μL) was taken, diluted 50-fold, and then stored at 4 °C. 75 μL of a 50-fold diluted CD solution plus 100 μL of a 175 mM Eu3+ solution was mixed and stirred for 10 min to form the CDs-Eu3+ ratiometric fluorescent probes solution.

First, 1000 μL TC solutions in the concentration range of 0⁓100 μM (0, 1, 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 75 and 100 μM) were added to 175 μL CDs-Eu3+ ratio fluorescent probes solution, respectively. After 10 min, the emission spectra were recorded under an excitation at 390 nm. Finally, the relationship between I619/I545 values and different TC concentrations was calculated.

Tap and lake water samples were taken as real samples to validate further the practical application of constructed ratiometric probes. Samples were filtered with a 0.22 μm filter membrane, and three concentrations of TC (5, 20, and 50 μM) were spiked to water samples for recovery calculations.

A filter paper-based test strip with a diameter of 1 cm was prepared. The filter paper was soaked in CDs-Eu3+ probes solution for 10 min and then dried at room temperature. Then, 200 μL sample solution was added dropwise onto the test paper. After drying, the color change was monitored under UV light at 365 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and recognition mechanism of fluorescent probes
ce:section.ce:para: CDs with yellow fluorescence are synthesized by a simple hydrothermal method using p-phenylenediamine as a carbon source and HNO3 as an auxiliary molecule. As shown in Fig. 2A, the CDs’ emission peaks at 545 nm under different excitation wavelengths, and the highest fluorescence intensity is observed at 390 nm. CDs are uniform monodisperse nanodots demonstrating the same resolved lattice stripes spacing of 0.23 nm (Fig. 2B and Fig. S1). Moreover, the clear crystal lattice fringes of a single dot hint a high crystallinity of CDs. To explore the optimal excitation wavelength, CDs-Eu3+-TC was scanned at different excitation wavelengths. In the presence of Eu3+ and TC, CDs-Eu3+-TC emits red fluorescence at 619 nm under different excitation wavelengths (Fig. 2C). However, the fluorescence intensity at 619 nm is the highest under 390 nm excitation. This is consistent with the Eu-TC complex emitting bright red light at 619 nm. EDS spectrum reveals that CDs-Eu3+-TC contains C, Eu, and O elements (Fig. 2D). This indicates that the obtained CDs-Eu3+-TC were successfully ligated.

The effects of NaCl concentration, Eu3+ concentration, pH, and reaction time on TC detection are investigated. Fig. 4AB shows that the I619/I545 values underwent minor changes after adding different concentrations of NaCl solutions, suggesting that TC recognition by CDs-Eu3+ is not affected by salt concentration. The effect of different concentrations (0⁓200 mM) of Eu3+ solutions on the I619/I545 ratio is shown in Fig. 4CD. The highest I619/I545 value is achieved at 175 mM Eu3+ solution concentration. Therefore, this concentration is chosen for the subsequent experiments. The solution pH affects the TC morphology in an aqueous solution, tuning the coordination of Eu-TC and its fluorescence intensity. Fig. 4 E and F illustrate that the best value of I619/I545 is obtained at pH 8.2 since TC mainly exists as TC− at pH = 8 to 10 [37,38]. Thus, these conditions are more conducive to Eu-TC complexes formation, enhancing the characteristic red fluorescence of Eu3+. The reaction time is also examined by monitoring the FL reaction (Fig. S3). The I619/I545 value increases rapidly up to 10 min and then gradually stabilizes, indicating the reaction completion after 10 min. Therefore, the optimal parameters for subsequent experiments are a Eu3+ concentration of 175 mM, a pH of 8.2, and a reaction time of 10 min.

The influence of complex metal ions and organic molecules is often encountered when performing real sample detection of TC. Therefore, selectivity experiments are one of the important factors in evaluating the performance of fluorescent probes. To further evaluate the selectivity of the CDs-Eu3+ system, other antibiotics, metal ions, and amino acids are added to the sensing system. No significant change in the fluorescence of the system is observed after introducing these analytes, except for TCs (TC and OTC), as shown in Fig. 6 A. Moreover, these interfering substances are added to the paper-based probes containing CDs-Eu3+. As illustrated in Fig. 6B, the test paper containing TC and OTC shows a significant response (reddening). At the same time, the other test papers' color change is insignificant, indicating good selectivity of the probes for TCs.

To explore the application of CDs-Eu3+ for TC detection, the study is extended to TC detection in tap and lake water samples. Firstly, different amounts of TC were added to the prepared tap water and lake water samples. Then, these samples were tested for fluorescence intensity using CDs-Eu3+. Further, the TC content in these samples was calculated and compared with the actual amount added. Table 1 illustrates that the recoveries of TC in the samples range from 85.43% to 97.53%, with relative standard deviations (RSD) of 0.44% to 2.60%.

Also, the HPLC method and the proposed CDs-Eu3+ method is compared to detect TC in real samples. Table 1 shows that the two methods had the similar recoveries. Moreover, the significant differences for both methods are statistically analyzed. Statistical data suggest no significant difference between the two methods with P > 0.05 (the proposed CDs-Eu3+ method and the HPLC method), indicating that the proposed CDs-Eu3+ fluorescence probes have great potential in practical applications [48]. The acceptable recoveries ± RSD values obtained for both methods indicates that the accuracy and precision of both methods are acceptable.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs -Eu3+ ratiometric fluorescent test strips for semi-quantitative detection of TC
ce:section.ce:para: The ratiometric fluorescent test strips embedded with CDs-Eu3+ are visualized for TC detection in water samples (Fig. S4). Water samples containing different TC concentrations are added dropwise, and the color of the test strip changes under UV light (365 nm). With the increase in TC concentration from 0 μM to 50 μM, the test strip color gradually changes to red. Therefore, the approximate TC content can be determined from the strip color change; thus, it can semi-quantitative detect TC in water samples rapidly. Moreover, these results are comparable to most reported methods, suggesting that CDs-Eu3+ exhibits promising application potential for TC detection.

In this research, the CDs-Eu3+ system is designed as a fast, sensitive, and selective fluorescent probes for TC detection. Adding TC to the system alters the fluorescence intensity of CDs and Eu3+. The method specifically recognizes TC with a detection limit of 31 nM. The primary advantage of the proposed CDs-Eu3+ method is that no modification of CDs or CDs-Eu3+ is required, significantly simplifying the process and reducing the cost. Moreover, this CDs-Eu3+ system can detect TC in tap and lake water samples. Also, there was no significant difference between the two methods (the proposed CDs-Eu3+ method and the HPLC method). Meanwhile, the paper-based probes embedded with CDs-Eu3+ can visually and semi-quantitatively detect TC. Undoubtedly, the CDs-Eu3+ system shows significant potential for TC detection in real samples.

Zhuoyuan Li: Writing – original draft, Validation, Data curation. Yulin Wang: Writing – original draft, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation. Jinting Chen: Validation, Data curation. Li Zhang: Data curation. Yongbiao Hua: Writing – original draft. Donglan Huang: Validation, Data curation. Huishi Guo: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Xiuzhen Qiu: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Social Development Science and Technology Collaborative Innovation System Construction Project of Shaoguan City (200811094530440, 220606144533513, 230329148030243), Guangdong Province Scientific Research Platform Project under Grant (2020KTSCX135), the scientific research project of Shaoguan University (SY2021KJ10), the 2023 Curriculum Civics Construction Project of Shaoguan University (21), the College Students Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Program (S202210576054, 202310576015) and the Special Fund for Science and Technology Innovation Strategy of Guangdong Province (Special Fund for the “Climbing Plan”) (pdjh2022b0475, pdjh2023b0478).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Efficient tetracycline removal from aqueous solutions using ionic liquid modiﬁed magnetic activated carbon (IL@ mAC)
